name: search
author: dojo-community
context: Search the APT cache, inspect package metadata, and explain candidate selection without changing system state
info: This skill covers non-mutating package inspection through `apt-cache` and related read-only commands. It searches package names and descriptions, shows metadata for exact packages, prints forward and reverse dependencies, reveals candidate selection with `policy`, and surfaces version tables with `madison`. Agents use it before installation, during upgrade analysis, and while debugging why a package is unavailable, pinned, phased, or selected from an unexpected source.
triggers: search for a package with apt

sections.title: Search package names and descriptions
sections.body: `apt-cache search` performs a full-text search over package names and descriptions. Add `--names-only` when you need tighter matching for automation or when descriptions create too much noise.

sections.title: Show package metadata
sections.body: `apt-cache show` prints the package record for a package, including dependencies, size, source, and descriptive metadata. Use `apt-cache madison` when you need a compact view of available versions instead of the full record.

sections.title: Inspect forward and reverse dependencies
sections.body: `apt-cache depends` shows which packages a given package requires or can use. `apt-cache rdepends` reveals which installed or available packages depend on it. This is essential before purging shared libraries or core tools.

sections.title: Explain candidate selection
sections.body: `apt-cache policy` explains why APT would pick a particular version. It displays installed state, the candidate version, and the priorities of each visible source. Use it to debug pins, target releases, phased updates, and third-party repository precedence.

links.context: Use inspection results to install or remove the right package
Understand why a source or version has higher preference
Add a source when the package is missing from current repositories
Use policy output to explain kept-back packages and phasing
Troubleshoot unavailable or conflicting package candidates

related.note: Policy output is the fastest way to validate whether pinning rules are working

scripts.name: Inspect package metadata
scripts.description: Search package metadata and explain version or dependency state

APT_SEARCH_ACTION.description: Inspection action: search, show, depends, rdepends, policy, madison, unmet, or list-installed

APT_PACKAGE.description: Single package name for show, depends, rdepends, policy, or madison

APT_PACKAGES.description: Space-separated package list for multi-package show, policy, or madison

APT_NAMES_ONLY.description: Set to true to search package names only

APT_RECURSE.description: Set to true to recurse dependency output

APT_INSTALLED_ONLY.description: Set to true to limit depends and rdepends to installed packages

query.description: Search term for full-text cache search

installedOnly.description: Restrict dependency output to installed packages

body:
# Searching and Inspecting Packages

Use this node when you need answers from the APT cache without changing package state. `apt-cache` works from the local metadata acquired by `apt-get update`, so stale indexes produce stale answers.

## Search and show

```bash
apt-cache search --names-only postgres
apt-cache show nginx
apt-cache madison docker-ce
```

`search` scans package names and descriptions. `show` prints the package records. `madison` is useful when you care about the exact versions currently visible from configured sources.

## Dependency inspection

```bash
apt-cache depends nginx
apt-cache rdepends libssl3
apt-cache unmet
```

`depends` shows forward dependencies and which packages can satisfy them. `rdepends` shows reverse dependencies. `unmet` summarizes unresolved dependency issues in the cache.

## Policy and candidates

```bash
apt-cache policy openssl
apt-cache policy
```

With a package name, `policy` shows the installed version, candidate version, and each source that contributes to the version table. With no arguments, it shows source priorities. This is the quickest way to understand unexpected candidates, pinning behavior, or phased updates. See [[apt/pinning]] and [[apt/upgrade#held-back]].

## Installed package listings

```bash
dpkg-query -W -f='${Package}\t${Version}\n'
apt list --installed 'python3*'
```

`dpkg-query` is the strict installed-state view. `apt list --installed` is often more convenient interactively.
